title-group.article-title: Microbiota as a modulator of drug response: targeting microbial–drug crosstalk in cancer therapy

abstract.title: ABSTRACT
abstract.p: In recent years, accumulating evidence has established mechanistic connections between the microbiota and the efficacy of anticancer treatments, encompassing microbial communities residing in the gut, within tumors, and across other tissue sites. Across diverse malignancies, distinct compositional and functional profiles of these microbial ecosystems have been associated with differential responses to chemotherapy, immunotherapy, and targeted agents, as observed in both clinical settings and experimental systems. It is now understood that microbial communities exert influence on drug activity through multiple pathways: they may compromise therapeutic efficacy by metabolic inactivation or structural modification of anticancer compounds, or, conversely, potentiate responses by enhancing tumor cell susceptibility. Furthermore, these communities modulate the tumor immune microenvironment, fostering antitumor immunity through augmented antigen presentation and effector cell function, or constraining immune responses via regulatory networks and immunosuppressive myeloid polarization. The crosstalk between microbial cues and signaling networks in malignant and surrounding stromal tissues is widely thought to influence therapeutic outcomes. Key challenges ahead involve proving causation, elucidating context-dependent mechanisms, and translating patient heterogeneity into robust biomarkers and targeted therapies. Anticancer interventions can reportedly alter microbial community structure and function, with therapy-associated dysbiosis potentially contributing to primary or acquired resistance. Microbiota-directed strategies, including transplantation, defined microbial consortia, targeted delivery systems, and lifestyle interventions, are under investigation, yet challenges remain in establishing causality, ensuring safety, and enabling patient stratification. Collectively, these insights establish a translational framework for rationally designed microbiota‒drug combination strategies aimed at enhancing anticancer therapeutic outcomes.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: The human microbiota comprises bacteria, viruses, fungi, and archaea that inhabit multiple body sites, including the gut, oral cavity, respiratory tract, urogenital tract, and skin. The gut microbiota (GM) is the largest community and exhibits substantial diversity in composition.1 Rather than serving only as a commensal companion, the microbiota acts as a functional partner that shapes host immunity, metabolism, and inflammatory signaling.2-4 When this community is disrupted, dysbiosis has been linked to cancer development and progression, affecting malignant growth, metastatic dissemination, and responses to anticancer therapies.5,6

Neoplasms are commonly divided into solid and nonsolid tumors. Nonsolid malignancies disseminate through the bone marrow and bloodstream, are not amenable to resection, and depend on drug treatment. Solid tumors are more often treated with combinations of surgery, radiotherapy, and systemic agents. Immunotherapy and chemotherapy remain the main drug-based approaches in modern oncology,7 and they have produced meaningful benefits in colorectal cancer (CRC), liver cancer, melanoma, lung cancer (LC), and other malignancies.8-10 Immunotherapy has reshaped cancer care by enabling durable responses in a subset of patients. For example, checkpoint blockade restores T-cell activity by inhibiting Programmed Death-1 (PD-1)/Programmed Death-Ligand 1 (PD-L1) or Cytotoxic T-Lymphocyte-Associated protein 4 (CTLA-4) signaling, while CD47-targeted therapies disrupt the “don't-eat-me” signal and promote macrophage-mediated tumor phagocytosis.11-13 Even so, many patients experience primary resistance or relapse after an initial response.14 Growing evidence links this variation in efficacy to differences in the gut and intratumoral microbiomes, which can influence how patients respond to treatment.

Current evidence indicates that the GM and intratumoral microbiota (IM) actively influence drug efficacy and resistance, and the IM has become a major focus in cancer research.12,15,16 Distinct bacterial communities are often found in tumors compared with adjacent normal tissues, supporting the view that intratumoral microbes can contribute to resistance and motivating efforts to define the underlying mechanisms.17-19 Microbial metabolites, including short-chain fatty acids (SCFAs) and secondary bile acids, can also shift treatment outcomes by shaping immune surveillance, affecting drug bioavailability, and altering tumor-related signaling programs.20 These findings suggest that engineering the GM, the IM, or metabolite profiles may help address therapeutic resistance (Figure 1). Microbiome-directed approaches now extend beyond antibiotics (ATB) and probiotics to include fecal microbiota transplantation (FMT), biomaterial-based delivery strategies, and lifestyle interventions, each with the potential to reshape microbial communities and improve drug response. In this review, we summarize mechanistic studies that support a causal role for the microbiota in anticancer therapy through host–drug–microbe interactions. We also discuss the coupled feedback in which microbes influence drug efficacy and toxicity, while anticancer agents and concomitant medications reshape microbial composition and function. Finally, we highlight key barriers and future priorities for translating microbiota-based interventions into clinical practice.

fig.label: Figure 1.

caption.p: Overview of microbiota–anticancer drug interactions and microbiota-targeted strategies. The schematic summarizes how gut and intratumoral microbiota influence responses to anticancer therapies across cancer types through three major routes: remodeling of the tumor immune microenvironment, regulation of tumor and stromal signaling pathways, and microbiota-derived metabolites that modulate drug activity and treatment responses. Microbial populations highlighted in the diagram are color-coded according to reported effects on therapeutic efficacy: red, enhancing; blue, inhibitory; and green, bidirectional (context-dependent effects with evidence not fully consistent across studies). Representative microbiota-targeting interventions are also shown. GM: gut microbiota; IM: intratumoral microbiota; FMT: Fecal microbiota transplantation. Created with BioRender (https://biorender.com).

fig.alt-text: A diagram shows microbiota anticancer drug interactions and microbiota targeted strategies across various cancer types.
fig.long-desc: The diagram illustrates microbiota anticancer drug interactions and microbiota targeted strategies. A central capsule labeled "Drugs" connects to "Microbiota" below it. The microbiota influences anticancer therapies through three routes: "Immune response", "Signal pathway", and "Microbiota metabolites". These routes are arranged in a circular flow around the central elements. The diagram is divided into sections, each representing a cancer type: Breast and ovarian cancers, Colorectal cancer, Melanoma, Lung cancer, Pancreatic cancer, Urinary system cancer, Liver Cancer, and Glioma. Each cancer type is associated with specific microbial populations, some labeled "G M" for Gut Microbiota and "I M" for Intratumoral Microbiota. Microbial populations are listed around the periphery, including Clostridium, Blautia, Akkermansia, Bifidobacterium, Bacteroides, Lactobacillus, Lachnoclostridium, Pseudomonas, Fusobacterium, Serratia, Escherichia, Streptococcus, Staphylococcus, Enterobacter, Ruminococcus, Subdoligranulum, Slackia, Parabacteroides, Phocaeicola, Prevotella, Megasphaera, Roseburia, Leuconostoc, Lactobacillus, Enterococcus, Fusobacterium, Bifidobacterium, and Alistipes. A legend indicates "Enhancing", "Inhibitory", and "Bidirectional" effects.

sec.title: Microbiota orchestrate tumor progression and regression
sec.p: The microbiota can either promote or restrain tumor development through multifaceted interactions with the host. Such effects have been reported across gut, intratumoral, and extraintestinal communities, and across tumor sites including the colorectum, lung, liver, and pancreas, involving both bacterial and fungal members. As the underlying mechanisms become clearer, these communities are also being considered as targets for therapeutic intervention.

sec.title: Microbiota promote tumor progression
sec.p: The microbiota can influence cancer biology, and cancer patients often show microbial patterns that differ from those of healthy individuals.21,22 In CRC, specific gut microbes such as Bacteroides fragilis and Fusobacterium nucleatum have been linked to tumorigenesis. Bacteroides fragilis can drive chronic inflammation, weaken the epithelial barrier, and perturb host signaling, whereas Fusobacterium nucleatum supports tumor cell growth and survival by altering immune responses. Together, these findings point to microbiota-driven effects on CRC progression through inflammation and immune regulation.23-26 In addition, Peptostreptococcus stomatis has been reported to activate oncogenic pathways, including MAPK signaling, and to foster a pro-inflammatory milieu that supports increased proliferation in CRC.27

Interest has expanded beyond the gut microbiota to the IM, and recent work has clarified how it can influence tumor progression.28 Microbes within the tumor microenvironment (TME) have been shown to shape local immune responses and, in turn, affect how patients respond to therapy.29-31 Tumor tissues also harbor diverse microbial communities, and their composition can vary with tumor site.32 In pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC), for example, fungal colonization was found to accelerate tumor progression, particularly due to the enrichment of Malassezia species, which activate the mannose-binding lectin (MBL) pathway to promote cancer progression.33 Recent studies also report that intratumoral fungi, including Malassezia globosa and Alternaria alternata, can promote tumor growth by triggering IL-33–dependent activation of TH2 cells and ILC2s, which supports immune suppression in the TME. Antifungal treatment has been shown to reduce the accumulation of these cells and decrease tumor burden, suggesting a potential role for antifungal approaches in PDAC.34

In addition to the GM and IM, microbial communities in other body sites, including the respiratory tract, oral cavity, and biliary tract, also contribute to cancer progression by modulating the immune system and promoting tumor dynamics.35-37 This suggests that microbial infiltration and expansion in tumor tissues are facilitated by tumor-induced immunosuppression, emphasizing the microbiota's role in shaping the cancer microenvironment.30,31

sec.title: Microbiota suppresses tumor progression
sec.p: Certain microbes have been linked to tumor suppression, raising the possibility that they could be leveraged therapeutically and warranting further study. In PDAC, microbiome variation has been associated with prognosis independent of medication use. A comparison of the IM between patients with short-term survival (STS) and long-term survival (LTS) showed that intratumoral microbial diversity predicts outcome. Saccharopolyspora, Pseudoxanthomonas, Streptomyces, and Bacillus clausii were more abundant in LTS patients than in STS patients, and these taxa were associated with increased CD8+ T-cell recruitment and activation, supporting antitumor immunity and longer survival. Together, these findings suggest that microbiome composition may serve as a prognostic indicator and a therapeutic entry point in PDAC.38

Microbial metabolites also shape cancer biology. SCFAs, including butyrate (Btr), propionate, and acetate, are generated during fermentation of non-digestible carbohydrates such as dietary fiber and contribute to intestinal homeostasis.39 In a CRC mouse model, Clostridium butyricum, a Btr-producing probiotic, was found to suppress tumor progression by inhibiting the Wnt signaling pathway and promoting the enrichment of a higher proportion of SCFAs-producing bacteria.40 Btr has additionally been reported to restrain tumor growth through multiple routes: activating TLR4 and downstream MAPK/NF-κB signaling to promote CRC cell death,41 dampening the PKM2-driven Warburg program to rewire glucose metabolism,42 and inducing ROS-linked autophagy in hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC).43 Clinically, reduced levels of Btr and its producers (especially Faecalibacterium) have been correlated with acute myeloid leukemia (AML) progression,44 and can promote inflammatory B-cell lymphoma via the TLR4/MyD88/NF-κB pathway.45 Another SCFA, propionate, was found to inhibit LC cell proliferation by modulating survivin and p21.29 These findings highlight the important role of microbial metabolites in regulating tumor progression and immune responses, suggesting that modulating the microbiome could be a valuable therapeutic strategy.

Evidence now supports causal links between specific microbes and multiple cancers, with microbial metabolites and antitumor immunity contributing to these effects. Beyond these pathways, interactions between microbiota-derived signals and treatment response are increasingly implicated in resistance to immunotherapy, chemotherapy, and targeted agents, with clear implications for efficacy and prognosis. We discuss these drug–microbiota interactions in detail in the later section on anticancer therapies and the microbiota.

sec.title: 3 The role of microbiota in immunotherapy
sec.p: The advent of immune checkpoint inhibitors (ICIs) has significantly improved the prognosis of many patients with refractory cancers, offering renewed hope. However, the clinical benefit remains limited to a subset of patients, as primary and acquired resistance mechanisms hinder their full efficacy. Increasing evidence highlights the GM and IM as key modulators of ICI responses. Mechanisms of microbiota-ICI crosstalk include shifts in specific bacterial populations, microbe-derived metabolites, remodeling of the immune microenvironment, and alterations in tumor-intrinsic cellular signaling pathways. Targeting these microbial interactions provides a promising strategy to overcome resistance and enhance patient responses to immunotherapy.

sec.title: Colorectal cancer
sec.p: Emerging clinical evidence suggests that GM can significantly impact the effectiveness of ICIs in CRC patients (Figure 2, Table 1). Fusobacterium nucleatum, a well-known anaerobic bacterium, is strongly linked to CRC progression and resistance to immunotherapy.46-48 High levels of Fusobacterium nucleatum colonization are associated with reduced progression-free survival (PFS) compared to patients with low bacterial burden.49 Additionally, specific microbiota, such as Prevotella sp. CAG:485 and Akkermansia muciniphila, are linked to better responses to immunotherapy, while Bacillus S24-7 and CAG:927 are associated with poorer efficacy.47 These beneficial strains enhance the effects of anti-PD-1 therapy through glycerophospholipid metabolism, which upregulates IFN-γ/IL-2, further suggesting that specific microbiota modulate immune responses to ICIs.50 Akkermansia muciniphila has been shown to enhance IL-2 immunotherapy across various tumor models, including mouse melanoma and colorectal cancer, with this effect partly mediated by its outer membrane protein Amuc via TLR2 signaling.51 Oral administration of Lactobacillus plantarum L168 has also been reported to boost anti-tumor immunity in CD8+ T cells in a CRC mouse model through its metabolite indole-3-lactic acid (I3A). I3A promotes IL-12 production by enhancing H3K27 acetylation in dendritic cells (DCs), opening the IL12a enhancer region, while simultaneously inhibiting Saa3 expression by modifying chromatin accessibility.52 In another study, oral administration of Lactobacillus rhamnosus GG synergized with anti–PD-1 monoclonal mAb, improving tumor control. This gut bacterium activates the cGAS/STING pathway in DCs, stimulating IFN-β–dependent CD8+ T cell infiltration into the tumor and amplifying antitumor immunity.53 Similarly, Lactobacillus acidophilus cell lysates enhanced the efficacy of CTLA-4 mAb by expanding CD8+ and effector memory T cells, while reducing Tregs and M2 macrophages in the tumor microenvironment.54 Together, these studies show that manipulating gut probiotics can rewire systemic and local immune circuits to potentiate ICI responses in CRC.

fig.label: Figure 2.

caption.p: Interactions and mechanisms of action between the gut microbiota and immunotherapy across different cancers. DCs: dendritic cells; anti-PD1 mAb: anti-programmed cell death protein 1 monoclonal antibody; IL-2: interleukin 2; ICIs: immune checkpoint inhibitors; ATB: antibiotics; IFN-γ: interferon gamma; TLR2: Toll-like receptor 2; CXCL16: CXC motif chemokine ligand 16; Treg: regulatory T cell; M2: M2-polarized macrophages; anti-CTLA4 mAb: anti-cytotoxic T lymphocyte-associated protein 4 monoclonal antibody; GPL metabolism: glycerophospholipid metabolism. This figure was created with BioRender (https://biorender.com).

Lactobacillus rhamnosus G G, D C s, C X C L 16, T L R 2, Lactobacillus acidophilus, L I P O A F, and Alistipes finegoldii are also present. Panel 4, lower left, shows Urinary system cancer. caption.p: The influence of GM and microbial metabolites on immunotherapy response in human and mouse models.

fn.p: In the “Effect” column, “+” indicates enhancement of drug efficacy, while “–” indicates suppression of drug efficacy. GM: gut microbiota, CRC: colorectal cancer, NSCLC: non-small cell lung cancer, PDAC: pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma, OC: ovarian cancer, RCC: renal cell carcinoma, HCC: hepatocellular carcinoma, ICIs: immune checkpoint inhibitors, PD-1: programmed cell death protein 1, PD-L1: programmed death ligand 1, CTLA4: cytotoxic T-lymphocyte-associated protein 4, Btr: butyrate, SCFAs: short-chain fatty acids, TMAO: trimethylamine N oxide, LPS: lipopolysaccharide.

sec.p: Alistipes finegoldii, a beneficial gut commensal, has been linked to prolonged survival in CRC patients, with similar associations observed in lung cancer and neuroendocrine tumors.55 In mouse models, Alistipes finegoldii enhances antitumor immunity and improves anti–PD-1 therapy efficacy in tumors with primary resistance to ICIs. This effect is driven by increased CD8⁺ T cell infiltration into the TME, with CXCR6 playing a critical role in recruiting highly cytotoxic, apoptosis-resistant CXCR6⁺CD8⁺ T cells. On a molecular level, the bacterial lipoprotein LIPOAF from Alistipes finegoldii binds directly to TLR2 on DCs, stimulating CXCL16 secretion and activating downstream antitumor immune responses. Both intravenous LIPOAF protein and oral administration of engineered Escherichia coli Nissle 1917 expressing LIPOAF significantly enhanced anti–PD-1 therapy efficacy. These findings highlight gut-derived LIPOAF/TLR2 signaling as a promising target for GM–drug crosstalk in adjunctive therapy, with a clinical trial (NCT05576961) already underway. Supporting the notion that gut communities can be engineered to modulate ICI responses, Tanoue et al. identified an 11-strain bacterial consortium from healthy human feces that synergized with anti–PD-1 therapy in a murine CRC model. This defined GM consortium greatly expanded tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes and, remarkably, was sufficient to suppress tumor growth on its own.84 These results suggest that gut-derived bacterial consortia can be engineered as living adjuvants to reshape host–drug–microbe interactions in CRC.

Furthermore, GM-derived metabolites are increasingly implicated in improved responses to immunotherapy (Figure 3, Table 1). In CRC, Btr has been repeatedly linked to better outcomes. Several Btr-producing species, including Clostridium butyricum, Agathobacter M104/1, Blautia SR1/5, and Roseburia intestinalis, have been associated with enhanced efficacy of immune checkpoint blockade.56,85,86 Mechanistic work in CRC suggests that Btr can potentiate anti–PD-1 treatment by increasing CD8+ T-cell infiltration, in part through MYC downregulation and suppression of MYC-dependent thymidylate synthase (TYMS), which may also help alleviate 5-fluorouracil (5-FU) resistance.85 In chemotherapy-refractory metastatic CRC, enrichment of Agathobacter M104/1 and Blautia SR1/5 has correlated with improved immunotherapy response, although the mechanisms remain unclear. Roseburia intestinalis has likewise been reported to restrain tumor growth and augment anti–PD-1 activity by promoting functional CD8+ T cells.56 Together, these studies support a role for Btr and other short-chain fatty acids in tuning antitumor immunity and improving CRC treatment outcomes.

fig.label: Figure 3.

caption.p: Interactions and mechanisms of action between gut microbiota metabolites and anticancer drugs across different cancers. Btr: butyrate; IL-12: interleukin 12; anti-PD1 mAb: anti-programmed cell death protein 1 monoclonal antibody; 5-FU: 5-fluorouracil; Th1: T helper 1 cell; Treg: regulatory T cell; CTL: cytotoxic T lymphocyte; ICIs: immune checkpoint inhibitors; GM: gut microbiota; UroA: urolithin A; 3-Oxo: 3-oxocholic acid; EMT: epithelial mesenchymal transition; TMAO: trimethylamine N-oxide; LPS: lipopolysaccharide; TLR4: Toll-like receptor 4; PDL1: programmed death ligand 1; TAMs: tumor associated macrophages; 3-IAA: indole-3-acetic acid; ROS: reactive oxygen species; DCs: dendritic cells; PD1: programmed cell death protein 1; TNF-α: tumor necrosis factor alpha; IL1β: interleukin 1 beta; TMZ: temozolomide; SCFAs: short-chain fatty acids. This figure was created with BioRender (https://biorender.com).

fig.alt-text: A diagram illustrates gut microbiota metabolites and anticancer drug interactions across various cancers, showing mechanisms.
fig.long-desc: The 2-panel diagram, arranged side-by-side, illustrates interactions and mechanisms of action between gut microbiota metabolites and anticancer drugs across different cancers. The left panel, titled "Metabolites," shows a central circular cluster of molecular structures. From this central cluster, multiple arrows extend to various microbial species and their associated metabolites, which then interact with different cancer types and drug treatments. For example, Clostridium butyricum, Blautia S R 1 slash 5, Roseburia intestinalis, and Agathobacter M 104 slash 1 produce Btr, which influences C D 8 plus T cells via I L 12 and G P R 109 a A K T pathways, affecting oxaliplatin and 5 F U. Bifidobacterium pseudolongum produces inosine, impacting T H 1 cells and anti C T L A 4 monoclonal antibody. L rhamnosus Probio M 9 metabolites affect T reg and C T L cells, influencing I C I s. Akkermansia muciniphila produces branched chain amino acids, affecting F O L F O X. Lactobacillus gallinarum produces indole 3 carboxylic acid, influencing T reg cells and I C I s. Prevotella 3 Oxo affects E G F R slash P E R K slash c M Y C and L O X signaling, influencing F O L F O X. Escherichia coli produces colibactin, affecting I F N gamma C D 8 plus T cells and E M T. Clostridium tetani, Clostridium tepidum, Enterococcus asini, and Enterococcus phoeniculicola produce T M A O, influencing I C I s and anti tumor immunity.

sec.p: Besides Btr, inosine, a purine nucleoside derived from adenosine, also plays a significant role in modulating immune responses and enhancing the efficacy of immunotherapy.57 Produced by specific GM, such as Bifidobacterium pseudolongum, inosine activates the A2AR-cAMP-PKA signaling pathway, which in turn promotes CREB activation, upregulates IL12Rβ2 and IFN-γ expression, and facilitates Th1 cell differentiation. These effects enhance the therapeutic efficacy of anti-CTLA4 monoclonal antibodies, as demonstrated in mouse models of small intestinal adenocarcinoma, bladder cancer, and melanoma.87 Recent metagenomics studies have shown that the gut probiotic Lactobacillus gallinarum is significantly depleted in CRC patient feces, suggesting that its loss may impair the host's antitumor defense. In CRC mouse models, oral administration of Lactobacillus gallinarum leads to rapid colonization and the production of indole-3-carboxylic acid, which suppresses the IDO1/Kyn/AHR axis. This suppression prevents CD4+ Treg differentiation and intratumoral infiltration, ultimately enhancing the efficacy of anti-PD-1 therapy in both MSI-high and MSI-low tumors.58

In addition to the GM, the IM play crucial roles in modulating immune responses to cancer therapies (Figure 4, Table 2). Tumor tissues from various cancers, including CRC, LC, breast cancer (BC), and liver cancer, have significantly higher bacterial loads compared to adjacent normal tissues, suggesting that tumors are more densely populated by bacteria.17 For instance, Fusobacterium nucleatum is notably more abundant in CRC tissues than in normal tissues or benign lesions.18 Fusobacterium. nucleatum, one of the key IM in CRC, has been shown to enhance anti-PD-1 therapy efficacy in microsatellite stable CRC (MSS-CRC) via Btr and the HDAC3/8-H3K27ac-TBX21-PD-1 signaling pathway.88 At the same time, commensal members of the IM can act as allies to enhance the efficacy of cancer therapies. For example, Bifidobacterium enrichment in CRC tumors activates the stimulator of interferon genes (STING) pathway, significantly boosting the effectiveness of anti-CD47 immunotherapy.12 Additionally, a proteomic study by Yao et al. identified bacterial peptides in CRC tissues, including Bacteroides fragilis-derived B2 and B5 peptides, that costimulate peripheral blood mononuclear cells and trigger strong immune responses.89 These findings suggest that IM can utilize unique bacterial peptides to enhance antitumor immunity, providing a novel approach to improving immunotherapeutic outcomes.

fig.label: Figure 4.
