sec.title: Liver cancer
sec.p: Although the liver is inherently sterile, it maintains a close molecular interaction with the intestinal microbiota via the gut–liver axis. In murine HCC models, bacterial translocation along this axis chronically activates LPS/TLR4 signaling, driving inflammation and carcinogenesis. In contrast, gut sterilization significantly reduces the incidence of HCC.98 With the increasing prevalence of both intrinsic and acquired resistance to first-line systemic therapies, the GM, acting through the gut–liver axis, is increasingly recognized as a key factor in determining drug efficacy and a potential target for therapeutic intervention99 (Figure 2, Table 1). Certain microbial taxa are closely associated with immunotherapy response and long-term survival of HCC patients. The enrichment of Akkermansia muciniphila, Ruminococcaceae, Lachnospiraceae bacterium-GAM79, Alistipes sp. Marseille-P5997, Ruminococcus calidus, and Erysipelotrichaceae bacterium-GAM147 correlates with enhanced antitumor immunity and longer OS and PFS. In contrast, the dominance of Proteobacteria or Veillonellaceae is associated with refractory disease and shorter PFS/OS.59,60 Recent studies have highlighted Phocaeicola vulgatus as a major driver of immunotherapy resistance in HCC. This bacterium is selectively enriched in nonresponders and impairs anti-PD-1 therapy efficacy in preclinical models by disrupting tryptophan metabolism and reducing microbial production of indole-3-acetic acid (3-IAA). The resulting shortage of 3-IAA weakens IFN-γ⁺ and granzyme-B⁺ CD8⁺ T cell responses. Exogenous supplementation of 3-IAA has been shown to restore cytotoxic T-cell activity and resensitize tumors to checkpoint blockade61 (Figure 3, Table 1). This microbe–metabolite–drug axis illustrates a bidirectional microbial–drug interaction, where the efficacy of systemic therapy depends on microbiota-derived metabolites, and therapeutic resistance can be reversed through metabolite supplementation. ATB exposure provides a complementary example of pharmacologic disruption of the microbiota–drug crosstalk. Broad-spectrum ATBs induce gut dysbiosis, depleting beneficial microbes and destabilizing the gut–liver ecosystem. Clinically, ATB-induced dysbiosis has been shown to impair therapeutic outcomes in HCC, increasing resistance to the standard combination of atezolizumab and bevacizumab, and significantly reducing both OS and PFS.100 This demonstrates how nononcologic drugs like ATBs can indirectly affect responses to cancer therapies by altering the microbiota, further emphasizing the importance of considering the broader pharmacologic context rather than focusing on individual drugs.

Most of the evidence for interactions between the microbiota and immunotherapy comes from preclinical mouse models. Several microbes with known immunomodulatory effects, including Bifidobacterium pseudolongum, Clostridium scindens, various Lactobacillus species, and Roseburia intestinalis, have been documented mainly in animal studies. In contrast, human cohort studies have identified specific associations, such as between Fusobacterium nucleatum and Ruminococcus species and the efficacy of immune checkpoint inhibitors, although the exact mechanisms remain undefined in animal models. Only a few key species, such as Akkermansia muciniphila, Alistipes finegoldii, and Clostridium butyricum, have been validated in both human and mouse studies. This disparity in evidence underscores a significant translational gap: microbes that are effective only in animal models need validation in human cohorts, while species identified as beneficial or harmful in human studies should be further examined in mouse models to clarify their mechanisms, providing a solid foundation for microbiota-targeted adjuvant therapies.

Unlike the GM, which communicates with the liver mainly through the gut–liver axis, the IM within liver tumors represents a more localized form of microbial–drug interaction (Figure 4, Table 2). In advanced liver cancer, IM has been shown to influence immunotherapy efficacy by activating the LPS/TLR4–CXCL1/JNK/MAPK pathway, promoting epithelial–mesenchymal transition (EMT) and creating an immunosuppressive environment that limits the effectiveness of ICIs.101 Clinically, patients with a higher abundance of Pseudomonas species in their tumors tend to have better outcomes, suggesting that certain IM members may positively modulate checkpoint blockade responses.102 Enrichment of Streptococcaceae in HCC activates the NF-κB pathway, which upregulates peroxiredoxin-1 (PRDX1) and promotes excessive glycolysis, leading to increased lactate in the TME. This excess lactate significantly suppresses CD8⁺ T-cell cytotoxicity while raising the levels of immune checkpoint molecules such as TIM-3 and LAG-3, which impairs antitumor immunity and reduces the effectiveness of anti-PD-1 therapy.97 These findings suggest that while much of the current research on microbiota–immunotherapy interactions in HCC focuses on the GM, intratumoral communities also play a significant role in microbial–drug interactions. However, data on IM remain limited and largely correlative, emphasizing the need for more clinical and mechanistic studies to better understand how both GM and IM can be safely and effectively manipulated to improve immunotherapy outcomes in liver cancer.

sec.title: Melanoma
sec.p: GM has been closely associated with melanoma immunotherapy efficacy, resistance, and adverse events (AEs).8,65,103-105 A growing body of work indicates that GM and ICIs engage in bidirectional crosstalk (Figure 2, Table 1). On the one hand, certain commensals can potentiate antitumor immunity: Bifidobacterium species enhance DCs function and promote CD8⁺ T cell infiltration into the TME, thereby improving the efficacy of anti–PD-L1 therapy.62 On the other hand, ICIs themselves can reshape GM behavior. In preclinical melanoma models, ICI treatment promotes the translocation of specific gut species, such as Enterococcus faecalis and Lactobacillus johnsonii, from the intestine to tumor-draining lymph nodes, where they activate DCs and T cells and enhance antigen presentation and systemic antitumor responses.63 In a related mechanism, Enterococcus produces bioactive muropeptides via its SagA enzyme, which are sensed by host NOD2 and further augment the antitumor efficacy of ICIs.64 Together, these studies illustrate a dynamic GM–drug crosstalk in which ICIs alter microbial localization and signaling, while microbiota-derived molecules feedback to shape ICI responsiveness. Not all GM exert beneficial effects on melanoma immunotherapy. Helicobacter pylori (HP) infection impairs responses to ICIs by suppressing DCs activity and CD8⁺ T cell activation.65,66 Similarly, antibiotic-induced disruption of gut microbiome homeostasis blunts systemic immunity and undermines the therapeutic potency of ICIs, resulting in significantly shorter PFS.106 Mechanistic studies in mouse melanoma models have further identified the PD-L2/RGMb axis as a critical node of microbiota–drug interaction. In this context, commensal microbiota enhance responses to anti–PD-1/PD-L1 therapy by blocking PD-L2–RGMb interactions, downregulating PD-L2 and RGMb expression, and increasing the CD8⁺ T-cell to Treg cell ratio, thereby overcoming tumor drug resistance.107

Btr, which is often linked to antitumor effects in other settings, shows context-dependent associations in melanoma immunotherapy. One study reported that higher Btr levels were accompanied by reduced benefit from ipilimumab, with weakened antitumor immunity that was attributed to fewer memory T-cell clusters and altered T-cell function. In that work, Btr was proposed to impair DC maturation and interfere with CD28 signaling, a pathway required for effective CTLA-4–based therapy.67 By contrast, a recent murine melanoma study found that Btr supplementation improved responses to anti–PD-1 therapy, whereas lower Btr levels were associated with poorer treatment outcomes. In this setting, Btr increased histone H3K27 acetylation at the promoter regions of PDCD1 and CD28 in human CD8⁺ T cells, thereby promoting their expression and ultimately augmenting the antitumor effect of anti–PD-1 therapy.68 Together, these seemingly contradictory findings suggest that Btr participates in a nuanced metabolite–drug crosstalk in melanoma, in which its impact on treatment resistance and sensitivity may depend on the specific ICI target (CTLA-4 versus PD-1), treatment context and epigenetic programming of T cells. Further work is needed to clarify how Btr can be harnessed or avoided in tailored microbiota–metabolite–ICI combination strategies.

In melanoma, IM can shape antitumor immunity through T-cell metabolic changes and, in some settings, by supplying bacterial neoantigens (Figure 4, Table 2). A higher intratumoral abundance of Lachnoclostridium has been associated with increased CD8+ T-cell infiltration, suggesting that certain tumor-resident bacteria influence the local immune milieu and support antitumor responses.108 Lactobacillus reuteri has also been reported to migrate to and establish within melanoma lesions, where it converts dietary tryptophan into indole-3-carboxaldehyde (I3C). I3C then activates aryl hydrocarbon receptor (AhR) signaling in CD8+ T cells, elevates IFN-γ production, and improves responses to anti–PD-1 therapy.94 Additionally, bacterial peptides presented by tumor-resident bacteria, such as Lactobacillus reuteri, have been shown to function as tumor neoantigens presented by HLA class I and II molecules. This provides an exciting new class of potential targets for immunotherapy, expanding the repertoire of microbial–drug crosstalk that can be exploited to personalize and enhance melanoma treatment.109,110

sec.title: Lung cancer
sec.p: Akkermansia muciniphila, a key GM species, has emerged as an important prognostic biomarker in non-small-cell lung cancer (NSCLC). Its abundance correlates with improved clinical responses to ICIs, particularly anti-PD-1 therapy (Figure 2, Table 1). In NSCLC mouse models, Akkermansia muciniphila enhances PD-1 blockade efficacy by working with the immune system to reduce tumor growth. Mechanistically, this microbe contributes to TME remodeling by disrupting the CXCL3–PD-L1 axis, which mediates communication between cancer-associated fibroblasts and PD-L1+ neutrophils. This interaction helps reverse CD8+ T-cell exhaustion, further boosting the effectiveness of anti-PD-1 therapy.69-71 Both endogenous and supplemented Akkermansia muciniphila have been shown to influence cancer progression, highlighting its critical role in modulating immune responses through microbial–drug interactions.111 Gut-derived probiotics, such as Clostridium butyricum and Bifidobacterium, have been shown to enhance the effectiveness of ICIs in LC. Clostridium butyricum improves ICI efficacy in LC patients by restoring the gut microbiota balance often disrupted by therapies like proton pump inhibitors, leading to better prognosis.72-74 Similarly, Bifidobacterium has been found to boost the activity of ICIs and oxaliplatin in LC mouse models, potentially through increased IFN-γ production and enhanced antitumor immune responses.112 Ruminococcus species are common gut bacteria involved in carbohydrate fermentation and nutrient absorption, including the breakdown of complex polysaccharides like cellulose.113 A higher abundance of Ruminococcus has been linked to longer survival and better responses to ICIs in NSCLC patients from both U.S. and Japanese cohorts.114,115 However, a study of Chinese LC patients found that treatment responders had higher levels of Alistipes putredinis, Bifidobacterium longum, and Prevotella, while non-responders had increased Ruminococcus levels.116 These findings suggest that ethnic differences in immunotherapy response may be shaped by variations in gut microbiota composition, potentially influenced by diet or lifestyle factors. Further research is needed to understand how these differences affect cancer outcomes, highlighting the potential of microbiota profiling in personalizing therapies. ATB treatment has been shown to reduce the efficacy of ICIs, likely by inducing intestinal dysbiosis, which weakens systemic immune responses and alters how the microbiome influences immunotherapy outcomes.117-120 This disruption underscores the complexity of microbial–drug interactions. Regarding GM metabolites, clinical data show that NSCLC patients who respond to therapy have higher serum levels of SCFAs compared to non-responders.68 A recent prospective study of 106 advanced NSCLC patients receiving immunotherapy combined with cisplatin-based chemotherapy demonstrated that specific GM, such as Faecalibacterium and Subdoligranulum, influence immune responses and cytokine profiles through SCFAs (Figure 3, Table 1). In preclinical models, including the Lewis LC mouse model, these GM were shown to activate effector T cells via SCFAs, enhancing antitumor immunity and improving the efficacy of immunochemotherapy.75

sec.title: Pancreatic cancer
sec.p: Pancreatic cancer patients generally show poor responses to ICIs. However, preclinical studies suggest that coadministering the oral bacterium Megasphaera sp. XA511 with PD-1 blockade can enhance tumor suppression in PDAC models.76 Additionally, the microbial metabolite trimethylamine N-oxide (TMAO) plays a critical role in modulating ICI efficacy. 16S rRNA profiling has shown that responders to anti-PD-1 therapy are enriched in CutC-expressing strains, such as Clostridium tetani, Clostridium tepidum, Enterococcus asini, and Enterococcus phoeniculicola, as CutC is the key enzyme responsible for TMAO production. In PDAC models, TMAO supplementation enhances ICI efficacy by reshaping the tumor immune microenvironment. This includes the activation of immunostimulatory macrophages, amplification of type I interferon signaling, and activation of effector T cells, leading to reduced tumor growth and prolonged survival.77 In the orthotopic murine PDAC model, disruption of the intestinal barrier has been linked to increased levels of lipopolysaccharide (LPS), a component of the Gram-negative bacterial cell wall, found in both the bloodstream and tumor tissue. LPS activates the TLR4/MyD88/AKT/NF-κB signaling pathway, directly driving the transcription of PD-L1, resulting in its increased expression on tumor cells. While LPS alone can promote tumor growth by impairing T-cell function, its upregulation of PD-L1 becomes a therapeutic target when combined with PD-L1 blockade therapy. Under these conditions, inhibiting PD-L1 can quickly reverse T-cell exhaustion and shift an immunosuppressive environment toward antitumor activity, demonstrating the crosstalk between microbiota and drug therapy in cancer treatment.78 IM have been shown to influence immunotherapy responses in PDAC (Figure 4, Table 2).121 In PDAC mouse models, depletion of the Col1 homotrimer increases T-cell infiltration and enhances the efficacy of anti–PD-1 therapy. This effect is linked to the inability of anaerobic Bacteroidales-enriched IM in hypoxic, immunosuppressive tumors to activate the Col1 homotrimer-α3β1 integrin signaling pathway, which is essential for driving oncogenic signaling, cancer cell proliferation, and tumor growth.96 These results suggest that both GM and tumor-resident bacteria can modulate immunotherapy effectiveness by reshaping the immune microenvironment through metabolic and signaling mechanisms.

sec.title: Urinary system cancer
sec.p: In renal cell carcinoma (RCC), enrichment of the gut bacterium Akkermansia muciniphila has been linked to better responses to anti–PD-1 therapy (Figure 2, Table 1). Mechanistically, Akkermansia muciniphila activates the MHC-II⁺ plasmacytoid DC–Th1 CD4⁺ T cell axis, increasing levels of CXCL9, CXCL10, and granzyme H, which enhances T-cell trafficking and cytotoxicity within the TME.69,79 In contrast, in immunologically “cold” prostate cancer, androgens promote a microbiome-mediated resistance program. The GM, like Clostridium scindens, convert cortisol into precursors of androgen receptor ligands, which supports proliferative signaling and reduces the effectiveness of treatment.80 In some ICI-responsive prostate cancer cohorts, reduced levels of Akkermansia muciniphila have been observed, suggesting that its effect on treatment response may depend on the context and tumor type, and be influenced by the endocrine environment.122 These findings indicate that both endocrine signaling and the GM participate in a hormone–microbe–drug interaction, which can either enhance or hinder immunotherapy in urologic cancers. In bladder cancer, clinical data further highlight the negative impact of antibiotic-induced dysbiosis. In the PURE-01 trial, prior antibiotic use significantly lowered the pathological complete response rate (15% vs. 50% in nonusers) and 24-month recurrence-free survival (63% vs. 90%), suggesting that antibiotic-driven disruption of the gut microbiota contributes to resistance to ICIs.123 Fecal 16S rDNA sequencing in bladder cancer patients revealed a reduced abundance of Parabacteroides. Supplementation with Parabacteroides distasonis in MB49 tumor-bearing mice, combined with anti–PD-1 therapy, significantly improved immunotherapy responses, slowed tumor growth, and increased intratumoral CD4⁺ and CD8⁺ T-cell infiltration.81 These findings suggest that restoring beneficial taxa like Parabacteroides distasonis may help counteract antibiotic-induced dysbiosis and re-establish a favorable microbial–drug interaction in bladder cancer.

Beyond the GM, the IM also influence immunotherapy responses in urinary system cancers (Figure 4, Table 2). In prostate cancer, the uropathogenic Escherichia coli strain CP1 has shown significant potential in reshaping the tumor immune landscape. After intraurethral administration, CP1 ascends from the urethra to the bladder, selectively colonizing prostate tumors without reaching the kidneys or causing systemic toxicity. In mouse models, combining CP1 with PD-1 inhibitors increases intratumoral CD8⁺ T cells, Th17 cells, mature DCs, M1 macrophages, and NK cells, while decreasing Treg cells and the pro-angiogenic factor VEGF, collectively overcoming resistance to PD-1 blockade.16 Taken together, these observations position both GM and IM as active participants in immunotherapy outcomes in urinary system cancers, providing multiple entry points for rational manipulation of microbial–drug crosstalk to enhance clinical benefit.

sec.title: Ovarian and breast cancers
sec.p: Ovarian cancer (OC) and breast cancer (BC) are generally considered low-immunogenic tumors, with only a subset of patients benefiting from ICIs. Recent studies suggest that both GM and IM, along with their metabolites, can help “activate” these tumors by reshaping T-cell responses and the tumor immune microenvironment, influencing the effectiveness of immunotherapy (Figure 2, Table 1). In OC, evidence is growing that the GM and its metabolites can modulate responses to ICIs and chemotherapy (Figure 3, Table 1). For instance, in OC patients who respond to ICIs, 16S rRNA sequencing of fecal samples has identified an enrichment of Ruminococcus and Leuconostoc mesenteroides. These bacteria produce lactate, which enhances CD4⁺ and CD8⁺ T-cell responses and boosts the effectiveness of both immunotherapy and chemotherapy by promoting cytokine production.82 Metabolomic analyzes in these responders also show a distinct systemic metabolite profile, including elevated levels of lipids such as phosphatidylcholines and triacylglycerides or diacylglycerides, as well as amino acids like serine and several bacterial-derived metabolites.82 In OC mouse models, oral mannose supplementation targets Faecalibaculum rodentium in the gut, further influencing treatment responses. Its derived metabolites, propionate and Btr, promote histone acetylation, stimulating the expansion and differentiation of progenitor-exhausted CD8⁺ T cells, ultimately improving immunotherapy response.83 This suggests the potential of mannose supplementation in targeting specific microbial taxa to enhance OC immunotherapy outcomes.

BC is likewise often considered immunologically “cold”, with immunotherapy demonstrating meaningful benefit only in selected subtypes, particularly triple-negative BC. In this context, the immunological fate of BC is critically shaped by the IM. In animal models, the bacterial load in normal mammary tissue is significantly lower (1.7 × 10⁴ bacterial equivalents per gram) compared to tumor tissue, where the bacterial load is nearly ten times higher (1.35 × 10⁵ bacterial equivalents per gram).19 Fusobacterium nucleatum uses its Fap2 lectin to bind Gal-GalNAc moieties on tumor cells, facilitating colonization and establishing a niche that excludes CD4⁺ and CD8⁺ T cell infiltration, thereby accelerating tumor progression and metastasis.124 In contrast, Achromobacter yanoikuyae, which is markedly depleted in cancerous tissue, can reactivate antitumor immunity by deploying glycolipid ligands that activate CD1d-restricted invariant natural killer T (iNKT) cells.125 From a translational perspective, tumor-associated Clostridiales species elevate systemic levels of the microbial metabolite TMAO, which triggers PERK-mediated endoplasmic reticulum stress, remodels the immune microenvironment and significantly potentiates immunotherapy efficacy.30 Together, these observations suggest that intratumoral bacteria and their metabolites form a BC-specific microbial–drug crosstalk axis that can either suppress or restore antitumor immunity, offering tractable metabolic and microbial entry points to overcome immune checkpoint blockade resistance (Figure 4, Table 2).

sec.title: Esophageal cancer
sec.p: In many solid tumors, GM features linked to ICI response are being progressively mapped, but comparable data in esophageal squamous cell carcinoma (ESCC) are limited. IM in ESCC, however, has begun to draw attention, and emerging evidence suggests that certain taxa may influence treatment response (Figure 4, Table 2). The enrichment of Streptococcus within ESCC lesions has been associated with higher CD8⁺ T-cell infiltration and better outcomes with anti–PD-1 therapy, suggesting that some IM members may increase local immune activity in tumors that are otherwise weakly immunogenic.92 By contrast, a higher intratumoral burden of Fusobacterium nucleatum correlates with poorer response to neoadjuvant chemotherapy, and greater bacterial abundance has been linked to treatment failure.93 These findings support a model in which intratumoral bacteria participate in microbe–drug interactions that can either favor ICI benefit or promote chemoresistance, and they suggest that selectively reducing detrimental taxa such as Fusobacterium nucleatum may improve outcomes.

Recent studies on microbiota and immunotherapy interactions mostly come from preclinical mouse models, where GM species like Bifidobacterium pseudolongum, Clostridium scindens, Lactobacillus, and Roseburia intestinalis have shown immunomodulatory effects. However, these findings are primarily based on animal studies, while human cohort studies have found associations between Fusobacterium nucleatum and Ruminococcus species and ICI efficacy, though the mechanisms are not fully understood. Additionally, IM such as Fusobacterium nucleatum and Bifidobacterium influence immune responses and tumor immunity in both animal and human studies. These microbes can either enhance or inhibit ICI efficacy by modifying the TME, often through metabolites like Btr. Notably, only a few microbes, including Akkermansia muciniphila, Alistipes finegoldii, and Clostridium butyricum, have been validated in both human and mouse models, bridging the gap between GM and IM in immunotherapy. These microbes affect both systemic and local immune responses, influencing the success of anti-PD-1 and anti-CTLA-4 therapies. The current gap in evidence highlights the need for further validation of microbes identified in animal models within human cohorts and for more exploration of human-associated microbes in mouse models. This dual approach will lay the groundwork for microbiota-targeted therapies.

sec.title: 4 The role of microbiota in chemotherapy
sec.p: Microbiota, encompassing both the GM and IM, are increasingly recognized as influential modulators of chemotherapeutic responses. Building on earlier evidence that GM shapes patient outcomes through drug metabolism, immune regulation and barrier integrity, recent studies further demonstrate that IM can directly modulate drug sensitivity within the TME. In this chapter, for each cancer type, we first summarize GM-mediated effects on chemosensitivity, chemoresistance and treatment-related toxicity, and then outline the emerging contributions of IM.

sec.title: Colorectal cancer
sec.p: In CRC, specific GM can directly modulate the cytotoxic activity of chemotherapy (Figure 5, Table 3). Bacteroides species are Gram-negative commensals that contribute to carbohydrate breakdown, microbial homeostasis and host immune regulation. Multiomics analysis of CRC patients receiving 5-FU identified Bacteroides vulgatus as a driver of 5-FU resistance by upregulating DNA repair and nucleoside transport-related genes that support nucleotide biosynthesis, thereby enabling tumor cells to better withstand 5-FU-induced damage.126 Conversely, several probiotic strains have shown the capacity to restore chemosensitivity in resistant CRC. In experimental models, combining Lactobacillus plantarum supernatant with 5-FU inhibits cancer cell survival by upregulating caspase 3 and suppressing Wnt/β-catenin signaling, suggesting its potential as an adjuvant strategy to overcome 5-FU resistance.127 Likewise, Lactobacillus rhamnosus R0011 enhances the efficacy of capecitabine in colon cancer bearing mice, leading to significantly reduced tumor size, which is associated with increased Bax and caspase 3 expression and decreased Bcl-2 expression compared with untreated controls.128 In contrast to these beneficial effects, ATB treatment markedly reduces the antitumor efficacy of 5-FU in CRC mouse models by disrupting GM homeostasis and impairing the microbiota supported chemosensitive state.129

fig.label: Figure 5.

caption.p: Interactions and mechanisms of action between the gut microbiota and chemotherapy across different cancers. ATB: antibiotics; 5-FU: 5-fluorouracil; EOC: epithelial ovarian cancer; IFNγ: interferon gamma; IL-6: interleukin 6; TNF-α: tumor necrosis factor alpha; TMZ: temozolomide; Treg: regulatory T cells; AC-D: doxorubicin, cyclophosphamide, docetaxel regimen. This figure was created with BioRender (https://biorender.com).

fig.alt-text: A 7-panel diagram shows chemotherapy interactions with gut microbiota across cancers, including drug resistance and immune.
fig.long-desc: The 7-panel diagram, arranged in a circular pattern around a central chemotherapy infusion bag, illustrates interactions and mechanisms of action between gut microbiota and chemotherapy across different cancers. The central panel shows a chemotherapy bag with arrows pointing to 6 surrounding cancer types: pancreatic cancer, colorectal tumor, genitourinary tumors, lung cancer, glioma, and breast cancer. Each cancer type is associated with a panel detailing specific microbial interactions. The pancreatic cancer panel shows Lactobacillus paracasei, G M dysbiosis, hyperglycemia, C D 133 plus T cells, vimentin, K i 67, A T B, gemcitabine, and 5 F U, with drug resistance indicated. The colorectal tumor panel shows Bacteroides, 5 F U, A T B, Lactobacillus rhamnosus R 0011, capecitabine, L. casei Shirota, B. breve, gemcitabine plus cisplatin, and mechanisms of drug resistance and apoptosis involving nucleotide synthesis, D N A repairing, Bax, caspase 3, B c l 2, W n t slash beta catenin, and I L 6. The genitourinary tumors panel shows cisplatin, E O C, G M dysbiosis, A T B, C D 4 plus T cell, cancer associated fibroblasts, C D 8 plus T cell, D C s, Lactobacillus casei Shirota, and Bifidobacterium breve. The lung cancer panel shows cyclophosphamide, Lactobacillus, Akkermansia muciniphila, Enterococcus hirae, Barnesiella intestinihominis, P 53, F A S L, F A S, K i 67, I F N gamma, I L 6, T N F alpha, T reg, T h 17, I L 10, and I L 17, I L 22.

caption.p: The influence of GM and microbial metabolites on chemotherapy response in human and mouse models.

tr.td: Lactobacillus casei Shirota and Bifidobacterium breve151

fn.p: In the “Effect” column, “+” indicates enhancement of drug efficacy, while “–” indicates suppression of drug efficacy. GM: gut microbiota, CRC: colorectal cancer, NSCLC: non-small cell lung cancer, PDAC: pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma, BC: breast cancer, CoPEC: colibactin-producing Escherichia coli, Btr, butyrate, 3-IAA: indole-3-acetic acid, 5-FU: 5-fluorouracil, FOLFOX: a combined regimen of 5-FU, leucovorin, and oxaliplatin, FOLFIRINOX: a combination regimen of 5-FU, irinotecan, and oxaliplatin with folinic acid, TMZ: Temozolomide.

sec.p: Gut microbial metabolites represent a key mechanism through which GM influences chemosensitivity in CRC (Figure 3). The Btr has been shown to enhance the efficacy of multiple chemotherapeutic agents in ways that partially mirror its effects on immunotherapy. Through the GPR109a–AKT signaling axis, Btr suppresses the glucose transporter GLUT1 and the nucleotide synthesis rate limiting enzyme G6PD in tumor cells, thereby markedly potentiating 5-FU-induced apoptosis. At the same time, it activates an ID2 dependent interleukin 12 pathway that promotes cytotoxic CD8⁺ T-cell expansion and further augments the antitumor activity of oxaliplatin.130,131 Additionally, Btr synergizes with irinotecan to reduce tumor cell viability and increase apoptosis by increasing the BAX/BCL2 ratio and downregulating p21, p53 and β-catenin.132 Together, these data illustrate a metabolite-centered microbial–drug interaction axis in which Btr remodels both tumor intrinsic metabolism and antitumor immunity to overcome chemoresistance. Beyond Btr, other GM-derived metabolites also modulate chemotherapy response in CRC. Urolithin A (UroA), a microbial metabolite produced from dietary ellagic acid and ellagitannins, has been reported to enhance mitochondrial function and cellular longevity.154 In CRC models, UroA boosts the anticancer effect of 5-FU by arresting the cell cycle through cyclin A and cyclin B1, and by triggering apoptosis via caspase activation.133 The FOXO3–FOXM1 axis is a key regulator of tumor drug resistance, with FOXO3 acting as a tumor suppressor that inhibits FOXM1; impairment of FOXO3 leads to FOXM1 upregulation and chemoresistance.134,135 UroA can increase the sensitivity of CRC cells and mouse tumors to 5-FU and reduce resistance by upregulating FOXO3 and downregulating FOXM1.136 At the level of bacterial taxa, Akkermansia muciniphila has been shown to enhance the efficacy of first line CRC chemotherapy with FOLFOX (5-FU, leucovorin and oxaliplatin), potentially through dipeptides containing branched chain amino acids identified by metabolomic analyzes.137 However, not all GM–derived metabolites counter drug resistance; in some settings, they can instead foster a chemoprotective niche that favors tumor persistence. Using spatial metabolomics together with 16S rRNA sequencing, investigators observed an increased abundance of colibactin-producing Escherichia coli (CoPEC) in specimens from patients with right-sided colon cancer,138 and colibactin was linked to a glycerophospholipid-enriched tumor microenvironment with lower immunogenicity, accompanied by reduced infiltration of IFN-γ⁺ CD8⁺ T lymphocytes and an association with chemotherapy resistance.138 Consistently, studies using CRC mouse models and in vitro experiments have shown that CoPEC promotes epithelial mesenchymal transition and the emergence of highly drug-resistant cancer stem cells, further enhancing chemoresistance.139 In addition to CoPEC, Prevotella species, anaerobic Gram-negative bacteria that participate in the breakdown of complex plant polysaccharides,155 have also been implicated in reduced chemotherapy efficacy. In CRC models, Prevotella decreases the anticancer efficacy of first-line FOLFOX therapy (5-FU, leucovorin and oxaliplatin) through its metabolite 3-oxocholic acid (3-Oxo).140 Although 3-Oxo normally facilitates fat digestion and nutrient absorption, in the context of cancer therapy it counteracts the effects of FOLFOX and induces drug resistance by modulating the P-EGFR/P-ERK/c-MYC and LOX pathways.140 These studies illustrate that GM can also drive chemoresistance by generating metabolites that remodel the TME, suppress antitumor immunity and activate pro-survival signaling in tumor cells.

Beyond the GM, the IM are increasingly recognized for their role in modulating chemotherapy efficacy (Figure 4, Table 2). In CRC, a high abundance of Fusobacterium nucleatum within the tumor is strongly linked to chemoresistance. This bacterium activates the TLR4/MyD88/NF-κB signaling pathway, upregulates the autophagy-related gene BIRC3, and inhibits the caspase cascade, thereby preventing apoptosis and promoting resistance to chemotherapeutic agents like 5-FU and oxaliplatin.26,90 Interestingly, LaCourse et al. proposed that part of the effectiveness of 5-FU might stem from its ability to inhibit Fusobacterium nucleatum, counteracting the tumor-promoting effects of this IM species.26,90 In contrast, other intratumoral bacteria, such as Escherichia coli, can metabolize 5-FU into an inactive form, bypassing its anticancer effects and ultimately reducing treatment efficacy.91 Together, GM and IM in CRC are involved in a complex microbial–drug crosstalk network that regulates chemosensitivity, providing complementary targets for strategies designed to overcome chemoresistance.

sec.title: Lung cancer
sec.p: In LC, increasing evidence suggests that GM can influence the effectiveness of platinum-based chemotherapy (Figure 5, Table 3). A randomized controlled trial (NCT03158129) on operable NSCLC patients receiving neoadjuvant chemotherapy found that different treatment regimens resulted in variations in the major pathological response (MPR) rate.141 Patients achieving MPR showed higher levels of Akkermansia muciniphila and a reduced presence of proinflammatory, potentially harmful microorganisms, indicating that Akkermansia muciniphila may have synergistic antitumor effects that contribute to deeper pathological responses and improved prognosis.141 In LC mouse models, Akkermansia muciniphila has been shown to enhance chemotherapy sensitivity, as its combination with cisplatin suppresses tumor growth more effectively than cisplatin alone.142 This combination decreases Ki-67, p53, and FasL expression in tumor cells, while increasing Fas levels, thus mitigating cisplatin resistance at the cellular level.142 Additionally, it boosts proinflammatory cytokines such as IFN-γ, interleukin 6 (IL-6), and tumor necrosis factor α (TNF-α), lowers interleukin 10 (IL-10), and remodels the immune microenvironment by reducing immunosuppressive regulatory T cells and modulating Th17 cells.142 These findings suggest that Akkermansia muciniphila enhances chemotherapy efficacy through a dual mechanism involving tumor cell signaling alterations and immune reprogramming, illustrating GM–drug interactions in LC. Other GM species also contribute to chemotherapy-related immune modulation.143 Enterococcus hirae contributes to microbial balance in the mammalian gut, while Barnesiella intestinihominis colonizes the colon and can regulate immunity by activating intestinal γδ T cells to produce IFN-γ. Both species have been reported to enhance the efficacy of the chemotherapeutic drug cyclophosphamide by shaping antitumor immune responses: Enterococcus hirae migrates to gut draining lymph nodes and increases the CD8⁺ T cell to Treg cell ratio in tumors, whereas Barnesiella intestinihominis accumulates in the colon and triggers intratumoral γδ T cells to secrete IFN-γ.143 This cooperative microbial–drug crosstalk has been associated with prolonged progression-free survival in LC and ovarian cancer patients, although the precise mechanisms remain under active investigation. Moreover, chemotherapy is often combined with targeted therapies like erlotinib to improve outcomes in NSCLC. Erlotinib targets EGFR in EGFR-mutated lung cancer, enhancing treatment precision. Recent studies suggest that GM can influence the efficacy of erlotinib. Higher levels of Bacteroides ovatus and Bacteroides xylanisolvens are linked to better treatment responses. In mouse models, supplementing with these bacteria improved erlotinib's efficacy by increasing CXCL10 and IFNγ expression, suggesting that GM enhances treatment effects by boosting immune cell recruitment. This highlights the potential of GM in modulating the response to targeted therapies.

However, disruption of GM by antibiotics can have the opposite effect on chemotherapy outcomes. In Lewis LC mouse models, antibiotic administration disturbs GM balance and induces resistance to cisplatin.144,145 Transcriptomic analyzes revealed that ATB treatment upregulates vascular endothelial growth factor A (VEGFA) and downregulates BAX and CDKN1B expression in tumor cells, thereby attenuating the antitumor effect of cisplatin at the tumor cell level. In contrast, coadministration of Lactobacillus with cisplatin promotes the expression of key effector molecules such as IFN-γ, granzyme B (GZMB) and perforin 1 (PRF1) in CD8⁺ T cells, whereas ATB use reduces the levels of these molecules.144,145 These findings indicate that GM disruption by antibiotics can shift microbial–drug crosstalk toward a chemoresistant state, while targeted probiotic supplementation may help restore a chemosensitive immune landscape in LC.

sec.title: Pancreatic cancer
sec.p: In PDAC, GM perturbations can substantially alter the efficacy of standard chemotherapy (Figure 5, Table 3). In PDAC mouse models, diabetes-associated hyperglycemia induces GM dysbiosis that enhances resistance to gemcitabine and paclitaxel, in part by increasing CD133 positive cells and thereby enriching a drug-resistant compartment.146 Clinically, however, several studies indicate that targeted or empirical ATB use can improve outcomes in specific settings. In PDAC patients treated with gemcitabine or 5-FU, concomitant ATB use was associated with better prognosis compared with chemotherapy alone.147 A large-scale cohort study of elderly PDAC patients further confirmed that those receiving combined chemotherapy and ATB had an approximately 11% higher survival rate, whereas no significant benefit was observed in patients treated with chemotherapy alone.156 These observations suggest that bacteria can be critical drivers of chemoresistance in PDAC and that, in some contexts, eliminating key resistance-promoting taxa through broad-spectrum nonpenicillin β lactam ATB may partially rewire GM–drug crosstalk in favor of chemosensitivity, although the specific species responsible for this survival benefit remain to be identified. Consistent with observations in other cancers, gut probiotics can exert antiresistance effects in PDAC. In PDAC model mice, combining gemcitabine with Lactobacillus paracasei reduces the grade of pancreatic intraepithelial neoplasia and suppresses vimentin and Ki-67 expression, indicating attenuation of EMT and proliferation under chemotherapy.148 These findings support the concept that GM manipulation, whether through ATB or probiotics, can steer microbial–drug crosstalk toward a more chemosensitive state in PDAC. Microbial metabolites also play a central role in shaping chemotherapy response in PDAC.157 Mechanistically, this combination attenuates the proliferation of tumor-associated macrophages, reduces stromal production and thereby disrupts the desmoplastic barrier that normally supports PDAC progression.158 In parallel, the FOLFIRINOX regimen combined with indole 3 acetic acid (3-IAA), a tryptophan metabolite produced by Bifidobacterium, inhibits PDAC growth by promoting reactive oxygen species accumulation and suppressing autophagy; this effect depends on neutrophil myeloperoxidase-mediated oxidation of 3-IAA.149 These studies delineate a metabolite-centered layer of GM–drug crosstalk in PDAC, whereby microbial products remodel stromal and stress response pathways to restore chemosensitivity.

At the local level, IM in PDAC can directly inactivate chemotherapeutic agents. γ-Proteobacteria within pancreatic tumors express a long isoform of cytidine deaminase (CDDL) that metabolizes gemcitabine into an inactive form, thereby conferring marked chemoresistance (Figure 4, Table 2). Targeting this IM component with ATB such as ciprofloxacin has been shown to counteract this metabolic inactivation and partially restore gemcitabine sensitivity.95 This example illustrates an IM centered microbial–drug crosstalk in which tumor resident bacteria metabolically deactivate chemotherapy, but can also be pharmacologically targeted to reverse resistance.

sec.title: Glioma
sec.p: The GM interacts with the central nervous system and participates in regulating its development and homeostasis. Central nervous system tumors have become one of the leading causes of cancer-related death, with glioma being the most common and having an extremely poor prognosis. Current evidence indicates that alterations in microbiota composition or its metabolites can influence glioma growth by modulating the tumor immune microenvironment.159,160 Despite current multimodal treatment involving surgery, radiotherapy, and chemotherapy, the prognosis for glioblastoma patients remains very poor, and recurrence is inevitable. Temozolomide (TMZ) is the standard alkylating agent for glioma, but its high resistance rate (response rate less than 50%) severely impacts efficacy and prognosis, with no effective sensitization strategies currently available.

Recent studies suggest that GM composition and its metabolites influence TMZ sensitivity. A Phase I/II trial (NCT03174197) combining radiotherapy, TMZ, and anti-PD-L1 therapy analyzed fecal samples from GBM patients and found that changes in GM were associated with overall survival (OS), suggesting that GM-targeted interventions could serve as an essential adjunct to current treatments161 (Figure 5, Table 3). Furthermore, significant differences in GM composition have been observed between TMZ-sensitive and TMZ-resistant mouse models. In Chemotherapy-responsive models, Akkermansia muciniphila was enriched, while Alloprevotella, Muribaculum, and Desulfovibrio were relatively less abundant in resistant models. Notably, after TMZ treatment, the GM in sensitive mice enhanced systemic inflammation by increasing blood TNF-α and IL-1β levels through tryptophan metabolism. This inflammatory cascade promoted the accumulation and proliferation of CD8⁺ T cells and macrophages, thereby enhancing the efficacy of TMZ.162 Akkermansia muciniphila has been shown to work synergistically with TMZ, further improving its antitumor effects. Additionally, gut bacteria such as Bifidobacterium and Anaerotruncus have been found to cooperate with TMZ, enhancing its therapeutic impact by modulating immune responses.153 These studies highlight the dual role of GM and its metabolites in influencing glioma treatment outcomes, underscoring the importance of microbial–drug interactions. They also suggest that GM-targeted approaches could improve TMZ efficacy and help overcome resistance mechanisms in glioma.

sec.title: Breast cancer
sec.p: The GM plays a crucial role in influencing the effectiveness of chemotherapy in breast cancer (Figure 5, Table 3). In patients undergoing the AC-D regimen (doxorubicin, cyclophosphamide, and docetaxel), longitudinal microbiome analysis revealed significant changes in microbial communities over time. The Lachnospiraceae NK4A214 group, Lachnospiraceae UCG-005 group, Christensenellaceae R-7 group, and Marvinbryantia declined during chemotherapy but rebounded after treatment. Conversely, Proteobacteria and unclassified Enterobacteriaceae expanded markedly during chemotherapy and then fell below baseline levels, while Lactobacillus steadily increased throughout the entire treatment course.163 These changes indicate that chemotherapy significantly alters GM composition, suggesting a microbial–drug crosstalk in which chemotherapy drugs directly influence microbiota abundance and diversity. In metastatic breast cancer patients treated with capecitabine, fecal metagenomic analysis revealed that Blautia obeum was positively correlated with longer PFS, whereas Slackia was associated with shorter PFS, further emphasizing the role of specific GM taxa in chemotherapy outcomes.150 In this regard, chemotherapy is often combined with targeted therapies like trastuzumab in HER2-positive breast cancer. While chemotherapy broadly kills rapidly dividing cells, trastuzumab specifically targets the HER2 receptor, enhancing treatment efficacy. Recent studies suggest that GM can influence the efficacy of trastuzumab.164,165 Moreover, ATB impair immune responses by disrupting GM, reducing T-cell recruitment, hindering dendritic cell activation, and lowering IL-12p70 release.164 Consistent with this, clinical studiey shows that nonresponders to trastuzumab have reduced gut microbial diversity, with lower levels of Lachnospiraceae, Bifidobacteraceae, and others, compared to responders.165 This confirms that GM plays a direct role in regulating the efficacy of targeted therapies by modulating immune responses, highlighting the potential of GM in enhancing treatment outcomes. Although the precise mechanisms by which microbiota modulate BC chemoresistance remain elusive, microbial metabolites are believed to play a key role. For instance, UroA, identified as a specific substrate of the breast cancer resistance protein (BCRP/ABCG2), dose-dependently inhibits the transport of mitoxantrone, thereby promoting chemoresistance.166 These findings underscore the growing evidence of a microbial–drug crosstalk that critically shapes chemoresistance.

sec.title: Genitourinary tumors
sec.p: The GM also plays a significant role in chemotherapy resistance in genitourinary cancers (Figure 5, Table 3). In advanced urothelial carcinoma, a combination of gemcitabine and cisplatin with probiotics (Lactobacillus casei Shirota and Bifidobacterium breve) improves therapeutic outcomes through the gut–tumor axis. This combination increases infiltration of CD8⁺ T cells and DCs into the TME, while decreasing CD4⁺ T cells and cancer-associated fibroblasts, suggesting that probiotics can enhance chemotherapy responses by modulating the immune landscape.151 In contrast, in an epithelial ovarian cancer (EOC) mouse model, ATB treatment led to significant resistance to cisplatin. Mice treated with ATBs showed reduced tumor cell apoptosis, enhanced DNA repair, active angiogenesis, and an increased population of cancer stem cells compared to untreated controls.152

In summary, most studies on GM–chemotherapy interactions have relied on preclinical mouse models, with human studies primarily showing correlative data. Certain microbes, such as Akkermansia muciniphila, Enterococcus hirae, and Barnesiella intestinihominis, have demonstrated positive effects in both human and mouse studies. However, many potential probiotics, including Anaerotruncus spp., Bifidobacterium longum, and Lactobacillus spp., have mostly been observed in animal models, and their clinical efficacy remains unverified. IM, such as Escherichia coli and γ-proteobacteria, contribute to chemotherapy resistance in both animal and human studies by metabolizing drugs into inactive forms or promoting tumor progression. In particular, CoPEC (colibactin-producing Escherichia coli) resides in the TME and contributes to chemotherapy resistance by altering lipid composition and reducing tumor immunogenicity. In mouse models, CoPEC promotes resistance by inducing EMT and the formation of cancer stem cells. Human studies have identified microbial markers such as Bacteroides vulgatus, Blautia obeum, Slackia spp., and the CoPEC group, although the mechanisms behind these associations are still unclear. Future research should focus on bridging animal model findings to human applications and elucidating the causal relationships behind these associations.

sec.title: Microbiota-targeting strategies
sec.p: As the crosstalk between the microbiota and drugs is increasingly recognized as a critical factor influencing cancer treatment outcomes, microbiota-targeting strategies have emerged as a promising approach to improve therapeutic efficacy and overcome resistance. Several strategies have been explored to precisely target the microbiota in cancer treatment, including FMT, probiotics, prebiotics, drug delivery systems, lifestyle interventions, and even antibiotic use. While antibiotics are commonly used to treat infections, their effects on the microbiota are typically broader and less selective, often disrupting microbial balance. Research has shown that antibiotic treatment can sometimes reduce drug efficacy, although a few studies suggest that, in certain contexts, antibiotics might enhance therapeutic outcomes. These aspects have been discussed in detail in Sections 3 and 4 of this review. In contrast, strategies such as FMT, probiotics, prebiotics, drug delivery systems and lifestyle interventions allow for more targeted modulation of the microbiota. These approaches aim to restore or promote the growth of beneficial microbial communities, adjust metabolic pathways, and enhance immune responses—ultimately improving cancer treatment outcomes and reducing resistance. In the following section, we will elaborate on these strategies in more detail.

sec.title: FMT
sec.p: FMT has shown promise in enhancing the efficacy of pharmacological treatments by reprogramming the gut microbiota. This therapeutic procedure involves transplanting fecal material from healthy donors into the gastrointestinal tract of patients, aiming to restore a balanced microbial community. While FMT has demonstrated improvements in the effectiveness of anticancer drugs, particularly ICIs, its clinical outcomes remain heterogeneous and controversial (Figure 6).

fig.label: Figure 6.

caption.p: FMT-mediated gut microbiota remodeling and its impact on anticancer drug efficacy in different cancers. GI: gastrointestinal; CRC: colorectal cancer; TCR: T cell receptor; IL-12: interleukin-12; IL-8: interleukin-8; Th1: T helper 1 cell; ICIs: immune checkpoint inhibitors; NSCLC: non-small cell lung cancer; Anti-PD1 mAb: anti-programmed cell death protein 1 monoclonal antibody. This figure was created with BioRender (https://biorender.com).

fig.alt-text: A 4-panel diagram shows F M T mediated gut microbiota remodeling and its impact on anticancer drug efficacy in cancers.
fig.long-desc: The 4-panel diagram, arranged vertically, shows F M T mediated gut microbiota remodeling and its impact on anticancer drug efficacy in different cancers. The topmost panel illustrates the F M T procedure. A donor figure on the left provides a fecal sample, which is then administered to a cancer patient on the right via upper G I route using a vingastroscope and capsules, or lower G I route via colonoscopy. The second panel, labeled C R C, shows an intestine with a tumor. Up arrows indicate increased Bacteroides thetaiotaomicron and Bacteroides fragilis, Proteobacteria and Sphingomonadaceae. Down arrows indicate decreased Bifidobacterium, Actinobacteria, and Bacteroides ovatus. These changes lead to T C R clonal expansion and increased Punicic acid slash aspirin. The third panel, labeled Melanoma, shows a skin lesion. Down arrows indicate decreased Veillonella atypica, Enterococcaceae, Streptococcus australis, Collinsella, and Enterococcus. Up arrows indicate increased Bifidobacterium, Faecalibacterium prausnitzii, Bacteroides thetaiotaomicron, and Bacteroides fragilis. These changes lead to increased I L 12, which activates T h 1 cells, leading to I C I s. This also leads to immune environment restriction, increased I L 8 producing myeloid cells, and increased C D 8 plus T cell activity. The bottommost panel, labeled N S C L C, shows lungs. Up arrows indicate increased Bacteroides, Blautia, Akkermansia, and Faecalibacterium.

sec.p: In CRC, early-phase trials have shown that FMT can enhance responses to PD-1-based therapies. A randomized controlled trial (NCT04130763) combining oral FMT capsules from “responder-like” microbiota with anti-PD-1 treatment in advanced CRC patients led to improved clinical responses, increased α-diversity, and higher frequencies of IFN-γ⁺ CD8⁺ T cells.167 In mouse models, FMT from healthy donors shifted key taxa like Bacteroides thetaiotaomicron and Bacteroides fragilis, with metabolomic profiling identifying metabolites like punicic acid and aspirin as contributors to enhanced PD-1 blockade efficacy.168 FMT combined with anti-VEGF therapy and anti-PD-1 in MSM-CRC models significantly reduced tumor progression, associated with microbiota changes, including Proteobacteria and Sphingomonadaceae, and T cell receptor clonal expansion.169 These findings highlight FMT's potential to overcome primary resistance to PD-1-based therapies in CRC. Similarly, in melanoma, FMT has also shown promise in enhancing ICI efficacy. The MIMic Phase I trial (NCT03772899) reported a 65% objective response rate (ORR) in advanced melanoma patients receiving FMT combined with anti-PD1 therapy. After a median follow-up of 20.7 months, 80% of patients remained progression-free. Microbiome analysis revealed that microbial metabolism, particularly Btr metabolism and glycan degradation, contributed to early immune activation, boosting anti-PD-1 therapy efficacy.170,171 In PD-1-refractory melanoma, FMT improved response to Pembrolizumab, with post-treatment microbiome changes leading to increased CD8⁺ T cell activation and reduced IL-8-producing myeloid cells.172 Another study showed that patients receiving Microbial Ecosystem Therapeutics 4 (MET4) with anti-PD1 therapy had enriched microbiota linked to ICI responses, including Bifidobacterium, Collinsella, and Enterococcus.173 Additionally, FMT enhanced ipilimumab efficacy in melanoma by activating IL-12-dependent Th1 immune responses via Bacteroides thetaiotaomicron and Bacteroides fragilis.174 A recent randomized trial (NCT03353402) demonstrated that FMT reshaped both the intestinal and TME, improving gene expression profiles and cancer treatment efficacy.175

In independent cohorts of advanced melanoma and NSCLC patients, Faecalibacterium prausnitzii was associated with better clinical responses to ICIs. Oral administration of the EXL01 strain (Faecalibacterium prausnitzii) in MCA205 tumor-bearing mice restored antitumor responses to ICIs and enhanced T cell activation, without altering microbiota composition, suggesting direct immunomodulatory effects.176 FMT from ICI responders into gnotobiotic mouse models of lung cancer (LC) reduced tumor growth and increased antitumor cell phenotypes, with enriched genera including Bacteroides, Blautia, Akkermansia, and Faecalibacterium.114 A clinical trial involving 13 patients with digestive system tumors (NCT04264975) showed that FMT enhanced anti-PD1 therapy efficacy, even in resistant cases. Of the 16 enrolled patients, 6 (46.2%) achieved clinical benefit, including 1 partial response and 5 with stable disease. FMT also induced sustained microbiota changes, increased CTLs, and elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines. Prevotella merdae was detected in FMT responders' feces, promoting CTL infiltration and enhancing T cell activity, while suppressing tumor growth in mice.177 In summary, FMT improves clinical outcomes by remodeling the immune microenvironment, enhancing T cell function, and increasing beneficial microbiota. However, the molecular mechanisms remain unclear, and future clinical applications will require tailored FMT strategies for different cancer types. Despite promising results, a randomized controlled trial (NCT03817125) showed no clinical benefit from FMT with SER-401 (a Firmicutes-based spore preparation).178 Sequencing analysis of immune-related gene expression profiles revealed the activation of signaling pathways associated with increased resistance to anti-PD1 therapy in the SER-401 group.178 Elevated PD1 expression and the presence of Tregs and effector T cells coexpressing TIGIT and PD-1 suggested a TME shift unfavorable for antitumor immunity. The addition of vancomycin to the SER-401 regimen complicated the interpretation of results. Although the trial failed to meet its objectives, it underscores the complexity of modulating the tumor microbiota and the challenges in microbiota manipulation.

Current research indicates that FMT benefits only a subset of patients, with limited efficacy in those resistant to immunotherapy. Moreover, most studies involve transplanting human feces into mice and lack direct application to human subjects, making the evidence for FMT still preliminary and necessitating further research (Figure 6).

sec.title: Probiotics and prebiotics
sec.p: Probiotics are gaining increasing attention in the context of cancer therapy. Among them, species of Lactobacillus, Clostridium butyricum, Akkermansia muciniphila, and Bifidobacterium are currently the most intensively studied. As discussed above, these probiotics can directly enhance the efficacy of anticancer drugs, but their ability to modulate gut microbial homeostasis is equally important. By selectively promoting the expansion of beneficial commensals and/or suppressing potentially pathogenic taxa, they reshape the gut microbiota and thereby influence drug metabolism, antitumor immunity, and overall treatment response. For example, administration of Lactobacillus rhamnosus Probio-M9 to a CRC mouse model enhanced the efficacy of ICIs by modulating the gut microenvironment. This probiotic promoted the growth of beneficial gut microbes, including Lactobacillus and Bifidobacterium animalis, leading to increased production of beneficial metabolites such as Btr, and the systemic accumulation of α-ketoglutarate, N-acetyl-L-glutamate, and pyridoxine. Consequently, these changes promoted the infiltration and activation of cytotoxic T lymphocytes (CTLs) while suppressing the function of Tregs within the TME.179 Similarly, in a CT26 murine CRC model, oral administration of an exopolysaccharide derived from Lactobacillus plantarum YT013 (YT013-EPS-1) modulated the gut microbiota and enhanced the therapeutic efficacy of locally delivered cisplatin hydrogel, resulting in marked inhibition of tumor growth and attenuation of cisplatin-induced systemic toxicity.180 16S rRNA sequencing further showed that this probiotic intervention enriched short-chain fatty acid–producing beneficial taxa, including Roseburia and members of the Lachnospiraceae family, thereby synergizing with the antitumor activity of cisplatin.180 Probiotic-based platforms, such as Lactobacillus rhamnosus GG coated with a gallium-polyphenol network (LGG@Ga-poly), have been developed to selectively target intratumoral Proteobacteria and lipopolysaccharides, reshaping the IM and potentiating immune checkpoint blockade.181 This example demonstrates how probiotic-based platforms can eliminate harmful microbes while enhancing antitumor immunity.

In parallel, prebiotics provide fermentable substrates that selectively support the growth and function of such beneficial bacteria, forming an integrated microbiota-targeted strategy to optimize the therapeutic effects of anticancer agents. Substances that are selectively utilized by host microorganisms and confer health benefits are defined as prebiotics. Prebiotics are typically nondigestible carbohydrates, including inulin, fructo-oligosaccharides, galacto-oligosaccharides, resistant starch, and natural polyphenols. After reaching the intestine, they are selectively metabolized and fermented by gut microbes to produce beneficial metabolites, such as short-chain fatty acids, thereby promoting intestinal health and modulating immune responses. In the context of cancer, these compounds exert antitumor effects and can act synergistically to enhance the efficacy of anticancer drugs. In addition, prebiotics can enrich gut microbial taxa such as Bifidobacterium and Lactobacillus, probiotics that have been shown in various cancer settings to improve the therapeutic effectiveness of anticancer agents. For instance, formulating inulin as a colon-retentive oral gel enables localized modulation of the colonic microbiota, reshaping the microbial community and increasing the relative abundance of key commensal species and their SCFA metabolites.182 This, in turn, induces a systemic memory T cell response, expands the pool of IFN-γ⁺ CD8⁺ T cells, and promotes the accumulation of TCF-1⁺ PD-1⁺ stem-like CD8⁺ T cells within the TME, ultimately leading to a marked improvement in the therapeutic efficacy of α-PD-1 immunotherapy.182 Furthermore, in CRC, prebiotic nanoparticles combined with capecitabine not only promote the growth of beneficial gut bacteria but also increase SCFA production, enhancing antitumor immunity and extending survival in mouse models.183 Similarly, a lipid nanoparticle system (FLSK) engineered by modifying 5-FU-loaded liposomes with prebiotic xylan derivative Sxy and probiotic Akkermansia muciniphila-derived phospholipid effectively extended the intestinal transport and release of 5-FU, ensuring sustained high drug concentrations at the tumor site, resulting in significant suppression of tumor growth.184 These findings underscore the significant potential of probiotics and prebiotics in combination with anticancer therapies, highlighting their ability to modulate the gut microbiome, enhance drug efficacy, and improve therapeutic outcomes in cancer treatment.

sec.title: Drug delivery systems
sec.p: In addition to the previously discussed systems that combine probiotics, prebiotics, and drugs to create new delivery platforms aimed at promoting gut microbiome balance and enhancing drug efficacy in Section 5.2, microbiota-targeted drug delivery systems also utilize the unique distribution and functions of bacteria within tumors and the gut to enhance anticancer efficacy and overcome resistance (Table 4). These systems can be broadly categorized based on their mechanisms, including targeting pathogenic bacteria, modulating the tumor microbiome, enhancing drug delivery, and improving immune responses. For instance, the PTE@CDDP system is designed to target and kill Fusobacterium nucleatum within colorectal cancer tumors, thus alleviating bacteria-induced inflammation. This, in turn, helps to reverse chemotherapy resistance and inhibits tumor growth.185 Similarly, the PYT@ZIF8@siRNA nanoparticles target Porphyromonas gingivalis, a bacterium linked to the progression of oral squamous cell carcinoma, and, by eliminating both the tumors and intratumoral Porphyromonas gingivalis, synergize with PD-1 blockers to enhance antitumor effects.186 Furthermore, LTA-MSN, which utilizes lipoteichoic acid antibody-conjugated mesoporous silica nanoparticles, recognizes bacteria-rich regions within tumors and releases chemotherapeutic agents locally upon binding. This localized drug release enhances drug accumulation and antitumor activity while sparing healthy tissues.17 Moreover, the oxaliplatin-encapsulated hydrogels system, which encapsulates oxaliplatin in hydrogels, targets the oral microbiome, activates antitumor immunity, and improves chemotherapy efficacy by modulating microbial populations within the TME.187 In addition, dextran nanoparticles conjugated with azide-functionalized bacteriophages have been shown to effectively deliver irinotecan, suppressing Fusobacterium nucleatum growth while enhancing chemotherapy through microbial modulation.188 These studies highlight the potential of microbiota-targeted drug delivery systems not only to enhance drug delivery but also to reshape the TME, offering novel strategies to overcome therapeutic resistance and optimize cancer treatment (Table 4).

caption.p: Summary of related studies on the impact of drug delivery systems targeting microorganisms on the efficacy of antitumor drugs.

fn.p: LTA-MSN: lipoteichoic acid (LTA) antibody-conjugated mesoporous silica nanoparticles (MSN), LGG@Ga-poly: a novel material of Lactobacillus rhamnosus GG probiotics coated with a gallium-polyphenol network, PTE@CDDP: an oral tellurium-containing drug carrier, tellurium-containing polycarbonate complexed with cisplatin, Phage-dextran: azide-functionalized bacteriophage-conjugated dextran nanoparticles deliver irinotecan, PYT@ZIF8: A nanoparticle that synergizes with PD-1 blockers and eliminates intratumoral Porphyromonas gingivalis, Oxa-hydrogel: an oxaliplatin-encapsulated hydrogel.

sec.title: Lifestyle
sec.p: Lifestyle factors, such as diet and exercise, can significantly influence the efficacy of cancer therapies. Research has shown that these factors can impact the GM and immune responses, thus potentially enhancing the outcomes of cancer treatments. A study examining the impact of dietary sucralose on patients with advanced melanoma, NSCLC, and those receiving neoadjuvant chemotherapy found that sucralose disrupted the GM, reducing therapy efficacy. This effect was linked to decreased microbiota-accessible arginine, essential for immune function. Tumor mouse models showed that supplementing with amino acids or using FMT could reverse drug resistance, restore T-cell function, and improve both immunotherapy and chemotherapy outcomes.189 In contrast, a high-fiber diet enhanced the efficacy of both immunotherapy and chemotherapy. Melanoma patients on a high-fiber diet had improved PFS and higher levels of beneficial bacteria like Ruminococcaceae and Faecalibacterium. Mouse models confirmed that a high-fiber diet increased interferon-gamma-positive cytotoxic T lymphocytes (CTLs) in the TME, enhancing anti-PD-1 therapy response. Clinical trials in metastatic NSCLC patients showed similar benefits, with increased alpha diversity in the GM and enrichment of Alistipes and Bacteroides salyersiae. Metabolomic analysis revealed that the high-fiber diet promoted propionate production, which boosted immune responses by downregulating DNA repair mechanisms and upregulating adaptive immunity.190,191 Exercise also improves the efficacy of both immunotherapy and chemotherapy, particularly in melanoma. Exercise activates microbial one-carbon metabolism, increasing formate levels, which enhances cytotoxic CD8+ T cell (Tc1) function and improves treatment outcomes. Formate promotes Tc1 differentiation and tumor-specific immunity, with Nrf2 identified as a key mediator in this process. These findings suggest that exercise enhances both immunotherapy and chemotherapy by promoting Tc1-mediated antitumor immunity.192 The summary table of research on the impact of lifestyle on antitumor drugs is shown in Table 5.

caption.p: Lifestyle shapes the GM to modulate anticancer drug efficacy.

tr.td: Reduced efficacy and restored by amino acids or FMT
Better PFS and more IFN-γ-positive CTLs in tumor
Formate promoted Tc1 differentiation and ICIs efficacy via Nrf2

fn.p: GM: gut microbiota, FMT: fecal microbiota transplantation, ICIs: immune-checkpoint inhibitors, CTLs: cytotoxic T lymphocytes, Nrf2: nuclear factor erythroid 2-related factor 2.

sec.title: Perspective and conclusions
sec.p: The microbiota, encompassing both GM and IM, has emerged as an important regulator of tumorigenesis and therapeutic response. Across treatment modalities, microbial communities can either augment drug efficacy or blunt responses, and they can contribute to both the development and mitigation of resistance. Importantly, the microbiota is not a passive bystander during pharmacological intervention; it actively shapes host physiology and immune contexture in ways that influence outcomes. Conversely, anticancer therapies can remodel microbial composition and function, and these therapy-driven shifts may feed back to affect subsequent responses. Within tumors, treatment may reveal previously undetected taxa or expand pre-existing populations, and such IM changes can, in turn, modulate drug efficacy. Collectively, these observations support a bidirectional drug–microbiota interplay in which reciprocal constraints or synergy can shape overall therapeutic benefit.

Mechanistically, microbiota influence therapy mainly through immune regulation, metabolite production, and signal transduction. Microbial communities can remodel the tumor immune microenvironment by modulating antigen presentation, DC activity, and cytotoxic T-cell priming, ultimately promoting CD8⁺ T-cell expansion and tumor cell killing. In CRC, for example, certain GM states have been linked to cGAS–STING activation in DCs or other antigen-presenting cells, supporting T-cell priming and synergy with immunotherapy. Beyond immune modulation, microbiota-derived metabolites can reprogram the tumor immune landscape and reshape cellular metabolism, thereby influencing drug sensitivity. Notably, the same metabolite can have context-dependent effects across tumor types; For instance, Btr has been associated with beneficial effects in some settings (e.g. CRC and PDAC) yet potentially unfavorable associations in others such as melanoma, underscoring the complexity of metabolite-mediated regulation.

Despite rapid progress, context-specific—and sometimes opposing—microbiota effects complicate translation. The same taxon can exert divergent effects across therapies: in CRC, Fusobacterium nucleatum has been linked to improved ICI activity via immune modulation, yet it may also promote chemoresistance through autophagy- and apoptosis-related pathways. Microbiota associations can also vary across tumor types and clinical contexts: Akkermansia muciniphila has been associated with improved ICI responses in CRC, NSCLC, RCC, and HCC, whereas prostate cancer cohorts have reported contrasting patterns, including reduced abundance in responders. Moreover, outcomes are rarely dictated by a single organism; dysbiosis can shift the relative influence of beneficial versus harmful taxa, altering efficacy and resistance trajectories.

Additional barriers include limited reproducibility of microbiome signatures across cohorts and population-level variability, which constrain biomarker development and clinical interpretation. Nevertheless, microbiota-targeting strategies—such as rational antibiotic use, probiotics/postbiotics, FMT, improved delivery platforms, and lifestyle interventions—offer potential routes to enrich beneficial taxa or suppress detrimental ones and thereby improve treatment responses. However, most evidence remains preclinical, clinical datasets are still limited, and patient benefit is variable. Moving forward, clarifying causal microbiota–host–drug relationships, standardizing sampling and analytical pipelines, and validating findings across multiple cohorts will be essential to enable mechanism-guided, patient-tailored microbiota modulation and improve long-term outcomes.
